---
title: "Notes on Benchmarking Qwen 3.5 vs Gemma 4: Local Agentic Coding on an RTX 4090"
tags: ["LLMs", "Benchmarking", "Local LLMs", "Agentic Coding", "llama.cpp", "MoE"]
description: Benchmarking notes comparing Qwen 3.5 and Gemma 4 model families (both dense and MoE variants) for local agentic coding tasks on an RTX 4090, using llama-bench and single-shot prompting with Open Code.
date: 2026-04-05
permalink: /2026-04-05-benchmarking-qwen35-vs-gemma4/
---

[Gemma 4](https://deepmind.google/models/gemma/gemma-4/) was relased by google on April 2nd earlier this and I wanted to see how it performs against Qwen 3.5 for local agentic coding. This post is my notes on benchmarking the two model families. I ran two types of tests:

1. **Standard llama-bench benchmarks** for raw prefill and generation speed
2. **Single-shot agentic coding tasks** using [Open Code](https://opencode.ai) to see how these models actually perform on real multi-step coding workflows

## Models

| Model | Architecture | Quant | Size on Disk | Total Params | Active Params |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Qwen 3.5 27B | Dense | Q4_K_XL | 16.40 GiB | 26.90 B | 26.90 B |
| Qwen 3.5 35B-A3B | MoE | Q4_K_XL | 20.70 GiB | 34.66 B | ~3 B |
| Gemma 4 26B-A4B | MoE | Q4_K_XL | 15.95 GiB | 25.23 B | ~4 B |
| Gemma 4 31B | Dense | Q4_K_XL | 17.46 GiB | 30.70 B | 30.70 B |

- **The size on disk is without kv-cache**
- All four models were run with thinking mode enabled and quantized to **Q4_K_XL** using [Unsloth](https://unsloth.ai/) GGUFs on [llama.cpp](https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp).
- All benchmarks were run on April 3rd, 2026.
- **Hardware**: NVIDIA RTX 4090 (24 GB VRAM). 


## Standard Benchmarks with llama-bench

llama.cpp has a `llama-bench` utility that runs standard prefill and decode benchmarks. It is a quick way to get raw throughput numbers before doing anything more involved.

This is the command I used to run the benchmarks:

```bash
./llama.cpp/llama-bench \
  -m $MODEL_PATH \
  -ctk q8_0 -ctv q8_0 -fa 1 -b 2048 -ub 512 \
  -p 512,2048,4096,8192,16384,32768,65336 \
  -n 128 -r 3 -o md
```

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="llama-bench flags used"}

| Flag | Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `-ctk` | `q8_0` | KV cache keys quantized to 8-bit |
| `-ctv` | `q8_0` | KV cache values quantized to 8-bit |
| `-fa` | `1` | Flash Attention enabled |
| `-b` | `2048` | Batch size (max tokens processed per batch) |
| `-ub` | `512` | Micro-batch size (tokens processed per CUDA kernel call) |
| `-p` | `512,2048,...,65336` | Prefill token counts to sweep |
| `-n` | `128` | Decode (generation) tokens per run |
| `-r` | `3` | Repeat each test 3 times and report mean |
| `-o` | `md` | Output as markdown table |

:::

### Prefill Speed

| Context | Qwen 3.5 27B | Qwen 3.5 35B-A3B | Gemma 4 26B-A4B | Gemma 4 31B |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 512 | 3,037 | 6,666 | **8,597** | 3,100 |
| 2K | 3,069 | 6,674 | **8,710** | 2,992 |
| 4K | 3,025 | 6,633 | **8,733** | 2,925 |
| 8K | 2,957 | 6,524 | **8,443** | 2,811 |
| 16K | 2,841 | 6,308 | **7,961** | 2,614 |
| 32K | 2,632 | 5,920 | **7,097** | 2,304 |
| 65K | 2,290 | 5,273 | **5,917** | 1,869 |

### Generation Speed (tg128)

| Model | Architecture | Generation (tokens/s) |
|---|---|---|
| Qwen 3.5 35B-A3B | MoE | **165.84** |
| Gemma 4 26B-A4B | MoE | 164.38 |
| Qwen 3.5 27B | Dense | 45.88 |
| Gemma 4 31B | Dense | 44.42 |


### Notes on llama-bench Results

- As expected, the MoE models dominate both prefill and generation speed.
- Generation speed for the two MoE models is nearly identical (~165 tok/s). Same story for the two dense models (~45 tok/s). This emphasises the fact that generation is memory bandwidth bound not compute bound.

> These numbers are useful but they only tell you about raw throughput. They say nothing about how a model actually performs when it has to reason, make tool calls, write code, and iterate on errors.

## Part 2: Agentic Coding Benchmarks

### Why Single-Shot Prompting

Standard benchmarks measure how fast tokens move. But for agentic coding, what matters is: can the model take a complex instruction, break it down, use tools, write code, run it, debug failures, and deliver working output?

I used single-shot prompting for this. One prompt, fired once, and the model has to figure out the rest on its own through tool calls. No hand-holding, no multi-turn guidance. This is closer to how I actually use these models day-to-day: I describe what I want, hit enter, and let the model do its thing.

I picked two prompts at different complexity levels to see how each model handles simple vs multi-step workflows.

### Setup

I used [Open Code](https://opencode.ai) as the agentic coding frontend because it is easy to set up with a local llama-server backend. [Context7](https://context7.com) was configured as an MCP server, which lets the model fetch up-to-date library documentation and API docs during its run.

The llama-server was launched with:

- Flash attention enabled
- q8_0 KV cache (turboquant)
- Batch size 2048, micro-batch 512
- Thinking mode enabled for all four models
- Context size varied per model based on VRAM constraints (details in [Appendix A](#appendix-a-hardware-fit-and-server-config))

Speed metrics came from llama-server's `/metrics` endpoint (Prometheus counters). Token usage breakdowns came from the [opencode-tokenscope](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@ramtinj95/opencode-tokenscope) plugin. I restarted llama-server between model runs so the counters would not carry over.


### Prompt 1: Simple (httpx + pytest)

```
use context7 to look up the httpx library docs. then write me a python script
that fetches the post from https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/posts/1 and
prints the title. also write a pytest test for it, no mocks, hit the real API.
use uv run to run everything so we don't install anything in the current
environment. run the test and make sure it passes.
```

This tests the basics: can the model call Context7 to look up docs, write a simple script, write a real integration test (no mocks), use `uv run` for dependency management, and actually execute everything to verify it works.


### Prompt 2: Comprehensive (Nano Banana Image Generation + TDD)

```
use context7 to search for the latest google gemini image generation API docs.
I want you to write a python script that uses the google-genai SDK to generate
images using the gemini-3.1-flash-preview model (nano banana). use TDD
red-green methodology, write failing tests first then make them pass. do not
use any mock tests. use uv run to run everything so we don't install anything
in the current environment. test the script and if it works fine and generates
an image, then use this script to run image generation on the five prompts
given in prompts.json. save the images to an images folder, make sure the
folder exists, if it doesn't then create it.
```

This is a much heavier multi-step workflow. The model has to:

- Look up the Gemini image generation API docs via Context7
- Write a Python script using the google-genai SDK
- Follow TDD red-green methodology (write failing tests first, then make them pass)
- Use real API calls, no mocks
- Use `uv run` for dependencies
- Read `prompts.json`, generate images for all five prompts
- Handle file I/O (create output directory, save images)

The idea was to push beyond "can it write a script" into "can it manage a real multi-step development workflow end to end."


### Results: Gemma 4 26B-A4B (MoE)

**VRAM:** ~21 GB / 24 GB &nbsp;&nbsp;|&nbsp;&nbsp; **Context:** 256,000 tokens

**Speed and Token Metrics (llama-server /metrics)**

| Metric | Prompt 1 | Prompt 2 |
|---|---|---|
| Prefill tok/s | 4,338 | 4,560 |
| Generation tok/s | 135.5 | 134.4 |
| Total prompt tokens processed | 17,847 | 23,204 |
| Total tokens generated | 1,623 | 3,435 |
| Prompt processing time | 4.11s | 5.09s |
| Generation time | 11.98s | 25.56s |

**Session Metrics (tokenscope)**

| Metric | Prompt 1 | Prompt 2 |
|---|---|---|
| API calls | 10 | 13 |
| Total session tokens (for billing) | 126,833 | 206,185 |
| Cache hit rate | 87.8% | 89.9% |
| Estimated cost (API pricing) | $0.018 | $0.027 |

**Tool Usage**

| Tool | Prompt 1 Calls | Prompt 1 Tokens | Prompt 2 Calls | Prompt 2 Tokens |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| bash | 4 | 1,537 | 10 | 4,949 |
| write | 3 | 19 | 0 | 0 |
| read | 0 | 0 | 1 | 144 |

**Observations:**

- Blazing fast. 135 tok/s generation and 4.3K+ prefill is the fastest of the four by a wide margin.
- Most concise model by far: only 1,623 tokens generated on Prompt 1, 3,435 on Prompt 2. No wasted output.
- Zero reasoning tokens on Prompt 2 (100% assistant output), which is interesting since all other models used thinking heavily.
- On Prompt 2, it used bash exclusively for file creation (cat/heredoc instead of the write tool). Not ideal but it worked.
- **Needed 3 attempts on Prompt 2.** Despite being the fastest and most concise, it struggled with the multi-step instructions. This is the tradeoff: raw speed is great, but instruction-following quality took a hit.
- 25.6s total generation time on Prompt 2 is remarkable though. Even with retries, the wall-clock experience was still fast.


### Results: Gemma 4 31B Dense

**VRAM:** ~24 GB / 24 GB &nbsp;&nbsp;|&nbsp;&nbsp; **Context:** 65,336 tokens

Note: The 31B dense model maxes out VRAM on a single 4090. I had to drop the context size to 65K to maintain reasonable generation speed. At 256K, generation speed degrades progressively because the dense model must use all 31B parameters on every forward pass and the KV-cache eats into remaining VRAM headroom.

**Speed and Token Metrics (llama-server /metrics)**

| Metric | Prompt 1 | Prompt 2 |
|---|---|---|
| Prefill tok/s | 1,466 | 1,357 |
| Generation tok/s | 37.7 | 35.2 |
| Total prompt tokens processed | 16,618 | 25,070 |
| Total tokens generated | 2,903 | 5,968 |
| Prompt processing time | 11.34s | 18.48s |
| Generation time | 77.07s | 169.53s |

**Session Metrics (tokenscope)**

| Metric | Prompt 1 | Prompt 2 |
|---|---|---|
| API calls | 10 | 16 |
| Total session tokens (for billing) | 135,625 | 316,463 |
| Cache hit rate | 88.0% | 92.2% |
| Estimated cost (API pricing) | $0.023 | $0.041 |

**Tool Usage**

| Tool | Prompt 1 Calls | Prompt 1 Tokens | Prompt 2 Calls | Prompt 2 Tokens |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| bash | 4 | 2,193 | 8 | 7,002 |
| write | 3 | 19 | 4 | 257 |
| edit | 1 | 7 | 0 | 0 |
| read | 0 | 0 | 1 | 144 |
| skill (find-docs) | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1,416 |

**Observations:**

- **Got Prompt 2 correct on the first turn.** This is the key result. The dense model's reliability on the complex task was noticeably better.
- Prefill at 1,466 tok/s is 3x slower than the Gemma MoE. Generation at 37.7 tok/s is 3.6x slower. You feel it.
- Generated nearly twice the tokens as the MoE variant (2,903 vs 1,623 on Prompt 1), including 1,548 reasoning tokens. Heavy thinker.
- On Prompt 2, 97.5% of output tokens were reasoning tokens. The model was thinking hard throughout.
- 169.5s of generation time on Prompt 2 means nearly 3 minutes just for token generation. For interactive agentic work, this drags.
- Loaded the `find-docs` skill during Prompt 2 (1,416 tokens), showing it actively sought documentation.
- Context limited to 65K is a real practical limitation. You cannot fit long agentic sessions on this model with a single 4090.


### Results: Qwen 3.5 35B-A3B (MoE)

**VRAM:** ~23 GB / 24 GB &nbsp;&nbsp;|&nbsp;&nbsp; **Context:** 200,000 tokens

**Speed and Token Metrics (llama-server /metrics)**

| Metric | Prompt 1 | Prompt 2 |
|---|---|---|
| Prefill tok/s | 3,179 | 3,056 |
| Generation tok/s | 136.7 | 132.3 |
| Total prompt tokens processed | 16,145 | 92,375 |
| Total tokens generated | 7,564 | 32,904 |
| Prompt processing time | 5.08s | 30.23s |
| Generation time | 55.32s | 248.75s |

**Session Metrics (tokenscope)**

| Metric | Prompt 1 | Prompt 2 |
|---|---|---|
| API calls | 13 | 30 |
| Total session tokens (for billing) | 186,817 | 809,989 |
| Cache hit rate | 91.6% | 94.3% |
| Estimated cost (API pricing) | $0.021 | $0.070 |

**Tool Usage**

| Tool | Prompt 1 Calls | Prompt 1 Tokens | Prompt 2 Calls | Prompt 2 Tokens |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| bash | 3 | 1,502 | 17 | 21,657 |
| write | 3 | 19 | 6 | 529 |
| todowrite | 5 | 606 | 0 | 0 |
| edit | 0 | 0 | 4 | 180 |
| read | 0 | 0 | 1 | 144 |

**Observations:**

- Generation speed is excellent at 136.7 tok/s, essentially identical to Gemma 4 MoE. Both MoE models hit the same bandwidth ceiling.
- But this model is **extremely verbose**. 7,564 tokens on Prompt 1 (the simple httpx task!) and 32,904 tokens on Prompt 2. That is 5.5x more than Gemma 4 MoE on the same complex task.
- 86% reasoning tokens on Prompt 1. The model thinks a lot before acting.
- Used a `todowrite` tool (5 calls, 606 tokens) on Prompt 1 which no other model invoked. This is a structured planning tool. The model was explicitly planning before executing, which is methodical but adds overhead.
- Prompt 2 was the most intensive run of the entire benchmark: 30 API calls, 28 tool calls, 810K billing tokens. 21,657 tokens consumed by bash alone.
- **Got Prompt 2 correct on the 2nd turn**, better than Gemma 4 MoE (3rd turn) but still not first-try.
- 248.7s generation time on Prompt 2. At MoE speeds this would have taken ~12 minutes at dense 27B speeds. The MoE architecture saves it from being unusable, but the verbosity still costs wall-clock time.
- At $0.070 estimated cost on Prompt 2, this is 3.4x more expensive than the next most expensive run, purely driven by token volume.


### Results: Qwen 3.5 27B Dense

**VRAM:** ~21 GB / 24 GB &nbsp;&nbsp;|&nbsp;&nbsp; **Context:** 130,672 tokens

**Speed and Token Metrics (llama-server /metrics)**

| Metric | Prompt 1 | Prompt 2 |
|---|---|---|
| Prefill tok/s | 2,474 | 2,188 |
| Generation tok/s | 44.9 | 44.6 |
| Total prompt tokens processed | 15,043 | 24,385 |
| Total tokens generated | 2,867 | 11,824 |
| Prompt processing time | 6.08s | 11.14s |
| Generation time | 63.91s | 265.00s |

**Session Metrics (tokenscope)**

| Metric | Prompt 1 | Prompt 2 |
|---|---|---|
| API calls | 9 | 18 |
| Total session tokens (for billing) | 116,759 | 315,568 |
| Cache hit rate | 87.5% | 95.5% |
| Estimated cost (API pricing) | $0.017 | $0.023 |

**Tool Usage**

| Tool | Prompt 1 Calls | Prompt 1 Tokens | Prompt 2 Calls | Prompt 2 Tokens |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| bash | 4 | 1,226 | 10 | 4,322 |
| write | 3 | 19 | 3 | 131 |
| read | 0 | 0 | 1 | 144 |

**Observations:**

- **Got Prompt 2 correct on the first turn.** Same as Gemma 4 31B Dense. Both dense models nailed the complex task first try, while both MoE models needed retries. Interesting pattern.
- Most efficient session on Prompt 1: fewest API calls (9), fewest billing tokens (116K), fewest tool calls. Clean and focused.
- Generation at 44.9 tok/s is slower than MoE (~135 tok/s) but faster than Gemma 4 Dense (37.7). Noticeable but workable for interactive use.
- 130K context fits comfortably in VRAM. This is a practical sweet spot: enough context for long agentic sessions without maxing out the card. Compare this to Gemma 4 Dense which could only manage 65K.
- On Prompt 2, it generated 11,824 tokens (second highest after Qwen MoE's 32,904), with 72.9% being reasoning tokens. It thinks, but not as obsessively as the Gemma 4 Dense (97.5%).
- 95.5% cache hit rate on Prompt 2, the highest observed. 18 API roundtrips with heavy context reuse.
- Despite the longer generation time (265s on Prompt 2), the cost stays low at $0.023 thanks to aggressive caching.


## Summary Tables

### Speed Comparison

| Model | Arch | Prefill tok/s (P1) | Prefill tok/s (P2) | Gen tok/s (P1) | Gen tok/s (P2) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Gemma 4 26B-A4B | MoE | **4,338** | **4,560** | 135.5 | **134.4** |
| Qwen 3.5 35B-A3B | MoE | 3,179 | 3,056 | **136.7** | 132.3 |
| Qwen 3.5 27B | Dense | 2,474 | 2,188 | 44.9 | 44.6 |
| Gemma 4 31B | Dense | 1,466 | 1,357 | 37.7 | 35.2 |

### Token Efficiency

| Model | Arch | Tokens Gen (P1) | Tokens Gen (P2) | API Calls (P1) | API Calls (P2) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Gemma 4 26B-A4B | MoE | **1,623** | **3,435** | 10 | **13** |
| Gemma 4 31B | Dense | 2,903 | 5,968 | 10 | 16 |
| Qwen 3.5 27B | Dense | 2,867 | 11,824 | **9** | 18 |
| Qwen 3.5 35B-A3B | MoE | 7,564 | 32,904 | 13 | 30 |

### Task Completion (Prompt 2)

| Model | Arch | Correct on Turn | Tool Calls | Estimated Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Gemma 4 31B | Dense | 1st | 14 | $0.041 |
| Qwen 3.5 27B | Dense | 1st | 14 | $0.023 |
| Qwen 3.5 35B-A3B | MoE | 2nd | 28 | $0.070 |
| Gemma 4 26B-A4B | MoE | 3rd | 11 | $0.027 |

### Hardware Fit (RTX 4090 24 GB)

| Model | Arch | VRAM Usage | Max Context |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gemma 4 26B-A4B | MoE | ~21 GB | 256,000 |
| Qwen 3.5 27B | Dense | ~21 GB | 130,672 |
| Qwen 3.5 35B-A3B | MoE | ~23 GB | 200,000 |
| Gemma 4 31B | Dense | ~24 GB | 65,336 |


## General Impressions

A few things stood out to me from this exercise.

**Dense models were more reliable on the complex task.** Both Qwen 3.5 27B and Gemma 4 31B got Prompt 2 right on the first turn. Both MoE models needed retries. Two data points is not a conclusion, but it is a pattern worth noting.

**MoE speed advantage is real but verbosity can eat it up.** Both MoE models hit ~135 tok/s generation vs ~40-45 tok/s for dense. But the Qwen 3.5 MoE generated 32,904 tokens on Prompt 2, which means 248 seconds of generation even at MoE speeds. The Gemma 4 MoE was the only model that was both fast and concise.

**Qwen 3.5 27B Dense feels like the best overall pick for agentic coding on a 4090.** Here is my reasoning:

- Reliable: got the complex task right on the first try
- 130K context is a practical sweet spot: enough for long agentic sessions, does not max out the card
- 44.9 tok/s is slower than MoE but fast enough for interactive use
- Most efficient on the simple task (fewest API calls, fewest billing tokens)
- Only uses ~21 GB VRAM, leaving headroom
- In my initial pass analyzing the generated code quality, it seems noticeably better than the other models. I am still working through this analysis and will update this section with more detail.

The Gemma 4 MoE is the speed king and if you are doing high-volume, simpler tasks where first-try reliability matters less, it is hard to beat. But for the kind of agentic coding I do day-to-day, where I want to fire off a prompt and trust the model to get it right, Qwen 3.5 27B Dense is still my go-to.

**Caveat:** These are notes from a single benchmarking session with two prompts. I am not claiming any of this is statistically rigorous. Different prompts, different tasks, different days could give different results. Take it as one data point.


## Code Quality (WIP)

I am still analyzing the quality of the generated code across all four models. My initial impression is that Qwen 3.5 27B Dense produces noticeably cleaner and more correct code compared to the other three. I will update this section with specifics once I have finished the analysis.


## Appendix A: Hardware Fit and Server Config {#appendix-a-hardware-fit-and-server-config}

### llama-server Launch Config

Base config used for all models:

```bash
llama-server \
    --model $MODEL_PATH \
    --jinja \
    --host 100.80.101.103 \
    --port 8001 \
    --parallel 1 \
    --batch-size 2048 \
    --ubatch-size 512 \
    --cache-type-k q8_0 --cache-type-v q8_0 \
    --flash-attn on \
    --context-shift \
    --metrics
```

### Per-Model Overrides

**Gemma 4 models:**

```bash
--ctx-size 256000     # MoE (26B-A4B)
--ctx-size 65336      # Dense (31B) - reduced due to VRAM constraints
--temp 1.0
--top-p 0.95
--top-k 64
--min-p 0.00
```

**Qwen 3.5 models:**

```bash
--ctx-size 200000     # MoE (35B-A3B)
--ctx-size 130672     # Dense (27B)
--temp 0.6
--top-k 20
--chat-template-file $TEMPLATES_DIR/qwen35-chat-template-corrected.jinja
--chat-template-kwargs '{"enable_thinking":true}'
```

Note: Qwen 3.5 models used a corrected Jinja chat template and different sampling parameters than Gemma 4. Thinking mode was enabled explicitly for Qwen via the chat template kwargs. For Gemma 4, thinking is enabled by default.


## Appendix C: Instrumentation {#appendix-c-instrumentation}

### Tokenscope

I used the [opencode-tokenscope](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@ramtinj95/opencode-tokenscope) plugin to get per-session token breakdowns. Add `"plugin": ["@ramtinj95/opencode-tokenscope"]` to your `opencode.json`, then create a `/tokenscope` slash command in `~/.config/opencode/command/tokenscope.md`. After each run, typing `/tokenscope` dumps a full report.

### llama-server /metrics

llama-server exposes a `/metrics` endpoint (enabled with the `--metrics` flag) that returns Prometheus-format counters. The two key gauges are:

- `llamacpp:prompt_tokens_seconds` (prefill speed)
- `llamacpp:predicted_tokens_seconds` (generation speed)

These are cumulative across the session, so I restarted llama-server between model runs to get clean numbers.

### llama.cpp Installation

```bash
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install pciutils build-essential cmake curl libcurl4-openssl-dev libssl-dev -y
git clone https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp
cd llama.cpp && git pull origin master && cd ..
cmake llama.cpp -B llama.cpp/build \
    -DBUILD_SHARED_LIBS=OFF \
    -DGGML_CUDA=ON \
    -DLLAMA_CURL=ON
cmake --build llama.cpp/build --config Release -j24 --clean-first \
    --target llama-cli llama-mtmd-cli llama-server llama-gguf-split llama-bench
cp llama.cpp/build/bin/llama-* llama.cpp
```


## Appendix D: Gotchas {#appendix-d-gotchas}

### Qwen 3.5 MoE todowrite Parse Error

The Qwen 3.5 35B-A3B MoE model sometimes returned tool call arguments as a raw JSON string instead of a parsed object. This caused the `todowrite` tool to fail because Open Code expected `todos` to be an array, not a string containing an array.

Fix: a small plugin at `~/.opencode/plugins/todo-fix-plugins.ts`:

```typescript
export const TodoFixPlugin = async (ctx) => {
  return {
    "tool.execute.before": async (input, output) => {
      if (input.tool === "todowrite" && typeof output.args.todos === "string") {
        output.args.todos = JSON.parse(output.args.todos)
      }
    }
  }
}
```

This is a common issue with local models that do not always serialize tool arguments correctly. Worth keeping around for any GGUF model that struggles with structured tool outputs.

### Gemma 4 31B Dense Context Size

The 31B dense model does not fit a 256K context on a single 4090. I had to reduce to 65,336 tokens to maintain ~40 tok/s generation. You can push it higher but generation speed degrades as context grows because the dense model activates all 31B params on every forward pass and the KV-cache eats into VRAM.

### Sampling Parameter Differences

The two model families use different recommended sampling parameters. Gemma 4 uses temp 1.0 with top-k 64, while Qwen 3.5 uses temp 0.6 with top-k 20. I kept each family at its recommended settings rather than forcing the same params across all four, since the goal was to see how each model performs under its intended conditions.

### Qwen 3.5 Corrected Jinja Template

Qwen 3.5 models needed a corrected Jinja chat template (`qwen35-chat-template-corrected.jinja`) to work properly with llama-server. The default template had issues with thinking mode. If you are running these models, check whether your template handles the thinking tags correctly.
